one_line_takeaway: A broad, application-focused taxonomy survey of LLM attacks (jailbreak, prompt injection/leakage, optimized attacks, model theft) and defenses (prevention vs detection, watermarking) — useful as an organized map of the 2023-2025 literature, but it contributes classification and secondhand-quoted metrics rather than any original measurement.
key_finding: The survey's synthesized takeaways: (1) attacks converge on prompt manipulation, and automatic/optimized jailbreaks (self-optimizing loops) are more efficient and threatening than manual ones; (2) LLM-integrated applications inherit the same prompt-injection/leakage/model-theft attack surface as base models but with higher blast radius (user data, vendor IP, system parameters); (3) model theft is not fully preventable, so defense has shifted toward watermarking for post-hoc IP traceability rather than prevention; (4) the field lacks generalized defenses and standardized evaluation, with a persistent usability-vs-robustness and resource trade-off.
core_contribution: A systematic survey and taxonomy of attack and defense techniques for large language models (LLMs), authored by Liao et al. (Jimei University). The paper's OWN contribution is organizational, not experimental: it proposes a two-branch taxonomy that (1) classifies attacks into adversarial prompt attacks (jailbreak, prompt injection, prompt leakage), optimized attacks, attacks on LLM-integrated applications, and model theft; and (2) classifies defenses into prevention-based vs detection-based methods, further split into white-box vs black-box for model-theft watermarking. Its stated differentiator from prior surveys is a deliberate emphasis on the security of LLM-integrated APPLICATIONS (not just standalone models), plus a closing Section 5 that names five practical challenges and future directions (evasion resistance, poisoning robustness, adversarial-attack detection, lack of generalized defenses, and ethics/bias). Tables 1 and 2 compile, per surveyed work, the method/algorithm, evaluated model, dataset, and metric.
threat_model: As a survey the paper aggregates many threat models rather than assuming one. It explicitly scopes itself to the security of the LLM's core architecture and its applications, and deliberately excludes a deep treatment of the data/privacy layer (data poisoning, training-data/PII leakage are mentioned only as context). The adversary is typically a black-box querier of a deployed model or LLM-integrated app who manipulates prompts (jailbreak/injection), reverse-steals system prompts (prompt leakage), optimizes adversarial triggers via query-response feedback (optimized attacks), or extracts/clones a proprietary model via mass querying (model theft). Defenders correspondingly either prevent (input/output/internal processing, structured queries, self-reminders, prompt signing, watermarking) or detect (perplexity filtering, attention-based detection, multi-agent filtering).
limitations: (1) No original experiments, benchmark, or head-to-head evaluation the authors run themselves; every number is quoted from the cited work, so there is no controlled comparison across methods. (2) Tables 1-2 are sparse and inconsistent, with many missing cells ('\') and OCR/typo artifacts (e.g., 'vicula-v', 'text-davici-002', 'Fl similarity'), limiting their utility as a reference. (3) The taxonomy has acknowledged overlaps (prompt leakage is conceded to be arguably a form of prompt injection) and the authors admit selective coverage ('due to space constraints'). (4) Indirect prompt injection and the data/privacy layer are explicitly deferred rather than covered. (5) The five future-directions items are generic (meta-learning, safe RL, FGSM adversarial training, EWC, fairness-aware optimization) and not tied to quantitative gap analysis. (6) It is a 2025 preprint 'submitted to Elsevier'; some cited results are early-stage.

datasets_used: NOTE: the survey runs no experiments; these datasets appear in its Tables 1-2 as datasets used BY the surveyed papers, not by this survey.
AdvBench harmful-behaviors benchmark (used across ABJ, DeepInception, AutoDAN, QROA, and defense baselines)
WMT14 and IWSLT / IWSLT14 (machine-translation model-theft and watermark work)
open-prompt-injection benchmark and deepset prompt-injection dataset (Attention Tracker)
SST2, MIND, AG News, Enron, WikiText, ROCStories (EmbMarker / GINSEW watermarking)
quantitative_results: ALL numbers below are quoted by the survey from the cited primary works, NOT measured by this survey.
AutoDAN [36]: attack success rate (ASR) 97.69% on Vicuna-7B and 73.65% on Guanaco-7B, bypassing confusion defenses.
PRSA prompt-leakage [71]: ~30% ASR with a single query against 50 popular GPT-store models (most with leakage protection), using GPT-3.5 as the generation model.
Agarwal et al. multi-round prompt leakage [1]: leakage rate up to 99% on GPT-4 and Claude-1.3 in a two-round RAG scenario.
QROA [24]: ASR exceeds 80% across Vicuna, Falcon, and Mistral, black-box query-only.
SmoothLLM [49]: reduces ASR of the PAIR semantic attack on Vicuna and GPT-4 by a factor of 2, and reduces GPT-3.5 ASR by a factor of 29.
System-Mode Self-Reminder [65]: reduces jailbreak ASR on ChatGPT from 67.21% to 19.34% on the authors' constructed dataset.
StruQ [8]: lowers manual-attack success on Llama/Mistral to <2%, and reduces GCG-attack success from 97% to 58%.
Attention Tracker [22]: +3.1% AUROC on the open-prompt-injection benchmark and +10.0% AUROC on the deepset prompt-injection dataset vs baselines.
Wallace et al. translation imitation attack [58]: imitation models reach Test BLEU 65.6 / 67.7 / 69.0 against Google / Bing / Systran respectively for English-to-German (WMT14).
baselines_compared: The survey itself runs no benchmarking and therefore compares no baselines of its own.

It positions itself against prior LLM-security surveys (e.g., Das et al. 2025 ACM CSUR; Esmradi et al. 2023; Shayegani et al. 2023) by claiming stronger emphasis on LLM-integrated-application security.

Within surveyed works, baselines noted include PAIR (for SmoothLLM), GCG / Zou et al. 2023 adversarial suffixes (for StruQ and PPL detection), and standard prompt-injection detectors (for Attention Tracker).
